questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Old Mill Lot
For decades, the empty lot beside the old Marlow Mill looked like an easy place to build. It was flat, close to downtown, and large enough for small businesses or apartments. But when the town received a redevelopment grant, planners learned that the soil needed to be tested before construction could begin. The mill had once used solvents and metal dyes, and no one knew whether those materials remained underground.
The first test found several areas with lead and petroleum residue above safe levels for residential use. Some residents feared the discovery would end the project entirely. Instead, the town used the test results to create a more careful plan. The most contaminated soil would be removed and taken to an approved facility. Other areas would be sealed under pavement, walkways, or clean soil. The town also changed the design so that a proposed playground would be moved to the cleanest part of the property.
The testing process slowed the redevelopment by nearly a year, but it also prevented costly mistakes. If apartments had been built before the contamination was discovered, cleanup would have been more difficult and residents could have been exposed to unsafe conditions. The town’s environmental consultant explained that old industrial sites are not automatically unusable, but they require evidence-based planning.
By the next spring, the lot still looked rough. Fencing surrounded piles of soil, and orange flags marked sample locations. Yet the project had moved forward in an important way: the town now understood the land it wanted to reuse.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Soil testing helped the town plan a safer redevelopment of an old industrial lot.
choices.B: The town decided to abandon the old mill lot permanently.
choices.C: The old mill lot was safe for apartments without any testing.
choices.D: Residents asked the town to build only playgrounds and no businesses.

questions.correctAnswerText: Soil testing helped the town plan a safer redevelopment of an old industrial lot.
questions.explanation: The passage explains how soil testing revealed contamination and allowed the town to revise its redevelopment plan safely.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Old Mill Lot.”
Why was soil testing necessary before construction?

choices.A: The town wanted to know whether former mill materials had left contamination underground.
choices.B: The town needed to decide which flowers to plant downtown.
choices.C: Residents wanted to turn the mill into a museum immediately.
choices.D: The lot was too steep for workers to enter safely.

questions.correctAnswerText: The town wanted to know whether former mill materials had left contamination underground.
questions.explanation: The mill’s past use of solvents and dyes created concern about what remained in the soil.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Old Mill Lot.”
Which TWO actions were part of the town’s revised plan?

choices.A: Removing the most contaminated soil
choices.B: Moving the proposed playground to the cleanest part of the property
choices.C: Building apartments before reviewing the test results
choices.D: Leaving all petroleum residue in uncovered soil

questions.correctAnswerText: Removing the most contaminated soil | Moving the proposed playground to the cleanest part of the property
questions.explanation: Both actions are described as part of the revised plan.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Old Mill Lot.”
What can be inferred from the consultant’s statement that old industrial sites are “not automatically unusable”?

choices.A: Contaminated sites may still be reused if risks are identified and handled properly.
choices.B: Old industrial sites are always safe without cleanup.
choices.C: Soil testing is useful only after buildings are finished.
choices.D: Industrial history has no effect on redevelopment planning.

questions.correctAnswerText: Contaminated sites may still be reused if risks are identified and handled properly.
questions.explanation: The consultant suggests that old industrial sites can be reused with evidence-based planning.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Old Mill Lot.”
In the sentence “the town used the test results to create a more careful plan,” careful most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=cautious and thoughtful
questions.explanation: In context, careful means cautious and thoughtful because the town used evidence to reduce risks.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Old Mill Lot.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: The mill had once used solvents and metal dyes.
tiles.B: The most contaminated soil would be removed.
tiles.C: The testing process slowed the project by nearly a year.
tiles.D: The town built the playground before testing the soil.

targets.T1: Evidence of a reason for concern
targets.T3: Evidence of a cost or delay caused by testing

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=The mill had once used solvents and metal dyes.; T2=The most contaminated soil would be removed.; T3=The testing process slowed the project by nearly a year.
questions.explanation: The tiles match the reason for concern, the safety response, and the delay.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Customer Allergy Alert Procedure
To: Café Counter, Kitchen, and Catering Staff
From: Food Service Manager
Subject: Customer Allergy Alert Procedure
Starting October 1, all allergy-related customer requests must be entered into the order system using the Allergy Alert button. This procedure applies to dine-in, takeout, and catering orders.
When a customer mentions an allergy, the employee taking the order must select the Allergy Alert button before sending the order to the kitchen. The employee should then type the specific allergen, such as peanuts, shellfish, eggs, milk, wheat, or soy. Do not write general notes such as “special request” when the customer has identified an allergy.
Kitchen staff must read the allergy alert before preparing the order. If an item cannot be prepared with reasonable precautions, the kitchen lead must notify the counter staff before the customer pays. Staff should not promise that an order is completely free of cross-contact unless a manager has approved the statement.
This procedure does not replace conversation with the customer. Employees should repeat the allergy information clearly and ask whether the customer has questions. The purpose of the Allergy Alert button is to reduce missed information, improve communication between the counter and kitchen, and help customers make informed choices.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how staff should record and communicate allergy-related customer requests
choices.B: To tell customers that the café will no longer serve takeout orders
choices.C: To require kitchen staff to ignore order notes
choices.D: To promise that every food item is completely free of cross-contact

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how staff should record and communicate allergy-related customer requests
questions.explanation: The document explains how to enter allergy alerts and communicate them between the counter and kitchen.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Customer Allergy Alert Procedure.”
What should the employee taking the order type into the system?

choices.A: The specific allergen mentioned by the customer
choices.B: The customer’s home address
choices.C: The name of the employee’s favorite menu item
choices.D: A note saying only “special request”

questions.correctAnswerText: The specific allergen mentioned by the customer
questions.explanation: Employees are instructed to type the specific allergen rather than a general note.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Customer Allergy Alert Procedure.”
Which TWO statements are supported by the document?

choices.A: The Allergy Alert button applies to dine-in, takeout, and catering orders.
choices.B: Kitchen staff must read the allergy alert before preparing the order.
choices.C: Employees should promise every customer that cross-contact is impossible.
choices.D: Allergy requests should be written only as “special request.”

questions.correctAnswerText: The Allergy Alert button applies to dine-in, takeout, and catering orders. | Kitchen staff must read the allergy alert before preparing the order.
questions.explanation: Both statements are directly supported by the procedure.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Customer Allergy Alert Procedure.”
Why should the kitchen lead notify counter staff if an item cannot be prepared with reasonable precautions?

choices.A: So the customer can be informed before paying
choices.B: So the kitchen can delete the order without explanation
choices.C: So the customer can be charged for two meals
choices.D: So counter staff can stop using the order system

questions.correctAnswerText: So the customer can be informed before paying
questions.explanation: The document says the kitchen lead must notify counter staff before the customer pays.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Customer Allergy Alert Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for using the Allergy Alert button?

choices.A: “Starting October 1, all allergy-related customer requests must be entered into the order system.”
choices.B: “Do not write general notes such as ‘special request’ when the customer has identified an allergy.”
choices.C: “The purpose of the Allergy Alert button is to reduce missed information, improve communication between the counter and kitchen, and help customers make informed choices.”
choices.D: “Employees should repeat the allergy information clearly and ask whether the customer has questions.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The purpose of the Allergy Alert button is to reduce missed information, improve communication between the counter and kitchen, and help customers make informed choices.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains the purpose of the procedure.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Start a Volunteer Driver Program
The county should create a volunteer driver program for older adults who need rides to medical appointments, grocery stores, and community services. Public transportation is available in the county seat, but many smaller towns have limited bus service. A missed appointment or an empty refrigerator can become a serious problem for a person who no longer drives and has no nearby family.
A volunteer driver program would not need to replace taxis, buses, or medical transport services. Instead, it would fill gaps for routine trips that are difficult to schedule otherwise. Volunteers could use their own cars after completing a background check, insurance review, and short training session. Riders could request trips through a county coordinator, who would match them with approved volunteers.
The benefits would extend beyond transportation. Regular rides can reduce isolation because riders have a friendly contact and a reason to leave home. Grocery trips and pharmacy visits can help older residents stay independent longer. A similar program in a neighboring county reported that most rides were under thirty minutes, yet riders described them as essential.
Some people may worry about safety and liability. Those concerns are valid, which is why the program should include screening, clear rules, and county coordination. The existence of risk is not a reason to ignore transportation needs. It is a reason to design the program responsibly.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: The county should create a volunteer driver program to help older adults reach essential services.
choices.B: The county should eliminate all taxis and buses.
choices.C: Older adults should stop attending medical appointments.
choices.D: Volunteers should drive riders without training or screening.

questions.correctAnswerText: The county should create a volunteer driver program to help older adults reach essential services.
questions.explanation: The author argues that a volunteer driver program would fill transportation gaps for older adults.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Start a Volunteer Driver Program.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that the program would meet a real need?

choices.A: Many smaller towns have limited bus service.
choices.B: Volunteers could use their own cars.
choices.C: Screening and rules can reduce risk.
choices.D: Some rides in another county were under thirty minutes.

questions.correctAnswerText: Many smaller towns have limited bus service.
questions.explanation: This detail supports the claim that existing transportation options do not meet all needs.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Start a Volunteer Driver Program.”

choices.A: Background checks for volunteers
choices.B: Insurance review
choices.C: Removing county coordination from the program
choices.D: Allowing unapproved drivers to accept rides
choices.E: Requiring riders to buy a car

questions.correctAnswerText: Background checks for volunteers | Insurance review
questions.explanation: The author recommends both background checks and insurance review as safeguards.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Start a Volunteer Driver Program.”
How does the author respond to concerns about safety and liability?

choices.A: By saying the program should include screening, clear rules, and county coordination
choices.B: By claiming safety concerns are imaginary
choices.C: By arguing that volunteers should avoid all training
choices.D: By recommending that riders arrange trips with strangers online

questions.correctAnswerText: By saying the program should include screening, clear rules, and county coordination
questions.explanation: The author responds to safety concerns by recommending responsible program design.

The county office has three conference rooms and a copier near the front desk.
Many drivers prefer listening to music during long trips.

questions.correct: D1=A county survey found that one in five residents over age 70 had postponed an appointment because transportation was unavailable.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A county survey found that one in five residents over age 70 had postponed an appointment because transportation was unavailable.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the argument by showing a measurable transportation barrier.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Start a Volunteer Driver Program.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Some transportation needs are not fully met by existing services.
choices.B: Every older adult lives in the county seat.
choices.C: Medical appointments are never important.
choices.D: Volunteers can safely drive without any rules.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some transportation needs are not fully met by existing services.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that buses, taxis, and other services leave important gaps.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Borrowed Camera
Jonas had promised to photograph the neighborhood soccer final because his younger brother’s team had made it for the first time. The camera was not his. It belonged to his cousin, who had handed it over with a warning: “Use the strap, keep the lens cap on, and do not pretend you know more than you do.”
At the field, parents unfolded chairs along the sideline. Children ran in uneven lines, their jerseys too bright under the noon sun. Jonas lifted the camera and saw only blur through the viewfinder. He turned one ring, then another. The players became sharp for a second and then disappeared into motion again.
He considered switching to his phone. No one would blame him. The phone would be easier, and the pictures would be good enough. Then his brother looked over from midfield and raised both thumbs, as if Jonas had already done something impressive.
Jonas took a breath and remembered his cousin’s last instruction. Do not pretend you know more than you do. He walked to the team bench and asked Coach Rivera, who wore a camera around her neck at every game, how to freeze movement. She showed him the setting and made him practice on the players warming up.
Most of his pictures still leaned slightly or caught the ball a moment too late. But one photograph showed his brother just after scoring, both feet off the ground, mouth open in surprise. Jonas knew it was not perfect. He also knew it was the picture everyone would want.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Jonas must decide whether to ask for help and learn to use the borrowed camera.
choices.B: Jonas argues with his brother about joining the soccer team.
choices.C: Jonas tries to cancel the soccer final because of the weather.
choices.D: Jonas refuses to take any photographs at the game.

questions.correctAnswerText: Jonas must decide whether to ask for help and learn to use the borrowed camera.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Jonas’s difficulty with the camera and his choice to seek guidance.

questions.correctAnswerText: It would be easier than learning to use the camera.

choices.B: He wants Coach Rivera to take all the pictures for him.
choices.C: He no longer cares about his brother’s game.
choices.D: He plans to return the camera without using it.

questions.correctAnswerText: He is willing to admit what he does not know in order to improve.
questions.explanation: Asking Coach Rivera for help shows humility and a willingness to learn.

choices.A: He asks Coach Rivera how to freeze movement.
choices.B: He practices on players warming up.
choices.C: He decides that no one will blame him for using his phone.
choices.D: He keeps every picture perfectly straight.
choices.E: He refuses to look through the viewfinder again.

questions.correctAnswerText: He asks Coach Rivera how to freeze movement. | He practices on players warming up.
questions.explanation: These details show that Jonas is learning and applying advice.

choices.A: A successful moment made possible by effort and asking for help
choices.B: A reason to stop attending his brother’s games
choices.C: Proof that the camera was broken
choices.D: Evidence that every earlier photo was useless

questions.correctAnswerText: A successful moment made possible by effort and asking for help
questions.explanation: The final photograph matters because Jonas captured an important moment after seeking help and practicing.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Borrowed Camera.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Jonas sees only blur through the viewfinder.
tiles.B: Jonas asks Coach Rivera how to freeze movement.
tiles.C: One photograph shows his brother just after scoring.
tiles.D: Jonas throws the borrowed camera under the bench.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Reading a Water Bill
A monthly water bill can do more than show how much a household owes. It can also reveal patterns in water use. Many bills show current usage, previous usage, rate tiers, service fees, and sometimes a comparison with the same month from the previous year. Understanding these parts can help customers spot changes before they become expensive.
The usage number is usually measured in gallons or cubic feet. A sudden increase may have an ordinary explanation, such as watering a new lawn or hosting guests. However, it may also suggest a leak. A toilet that runs quietly or an outdoor faucet that drips can waste large amounts of water over several weeks. If usage rises sharply without a clear reason, customers should check fixtures and contact the utility if needed.
Rate tiers are another important detail. Some utilities charge a higher price per unit after a household passes a certain amount of use. This system encourages conservation and helps cover the cost of treating and delivering water during high-demand periods. A household that moves into a higher tier may reduce its bill by spreading laundry loads through the week, fixing leaks, or using outdoor water more carefully.
A water bill does not explain every situation. Weather, household size, and local rates all matter. Still, reading the bill carefully can turn it from a demand for payment into a useful tool for managing a household resource.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Water bills can help customers understand usage patterns, possible leaks, and costs.
choices.B: Water bills show only the amount owed and nothing else.
choices.C: Customers should ignore water usage if the bill is paid on time.
choices.D: Rate tiers are used only when a household has no outdoor faucet.

questions.correctAnswerText: Water bills can help customers understand usage patterns, possible leaks, and costs.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that water bills provide information beyond the amount owed.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading a Water Bill.”
What might a sudden unexplained increase in water usage suggest?

choices.A: A possible leak
choices.B: A lower rate tier
choices.C: A canceled utility account
choices.D: A smaller household

questions.correctAnswerText: A possible leak
questions.explanation: The passage says a sudden unexplained increase may suggest a leak.

choices.A: To charge a higher price after a household passes a certain amount of use
choices.B: To prevent customers from seeing previous usage
choices.C: To make every household pay the same amount every month
choices.D: To remove service fees from all bills

questions.correctAnswerText: To charge a higher price after a household passes a certain amount of use
questions.explanation: The passage explains that some utilities use higher rates after certain usage levels.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading a Water Bill.”

choices.A: “Many bills show current usage, previous usage, rate tiers, service fees, and sometimes a comparison with the same month from the previous year.”
choices.B: “A toilet that runs quietly or an outdoor faucet that drips can waste large amounts of water over several weeks.”
choices.C: “Weather, household size, and local rates all matter.”
choices.D: “The usage number is usually measured in gallons or cubic feet.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “A toilet that runs quietly or an outdoor faucet that drips can waste large amounts of water over several weeks.”
questions.explanation: This sentence shows how small leaks can become costly over time.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading a Water Bill.”

choices.A: It explains parts of a water bill, shows how to interpret changes, and notes limits.
choices.B: It tells a story about one family moving to a new house.
choices.C: It lists every water utility in the state.
choices.D: It argues that customers should stop using water outdoors.

questions.correctAnswerText: It explains parts of a water bill, shows how to interpret changes, and notes limits.
questions.explanation: The passage describes bill features, explains what changes may mean, and acknowledges other factors.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Community Blood Drive Reminder

(3) Walk-ins are welcome, but appointments help staff manage the schedule. (4) [D1], donors with appointments may spend less time waiting. (5) The registration table [D2] located beside the main entrance. (6) Volunteers will direct donors to the waiting area and answer basic questions about the check-in process.

questions.explanation: Appointments reduce waiting time as a result, and “will be located” is the correct verb phrase.

choices.C: Donors will be directed by volunteers to the waiting area, where questions about check-in are answered by them.
choices.D: Volunteers, who are volunteers, will provide direction and answers in a basic way.

questions.explanation: This sentence is clear and concise.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Add a Community Tool Shed at the Park
The parks department should add a locked community tool shed at Henderson Park for volunteer cleanup groups. Volunteers often arrive ready to remove weeds, spread mulch, or pick up litter, but they must bring their own rakes, gloves, buckets, and trash grabbers. This requirement discourages residents who do not own tools or cannot carry them on the bus.
A shared tool shed would make volunteer work easier and more inclusive. The department could stock basic tools, require sign-out, and assign a staff member or trained volunteer to check equipment after events. Tools would remain on site instead of being transported across town. Community groups could schedule cleanup mornings with less preparation, which may lead to more frequent care of the park.
Some people worry that tools will be lost, broken, or stolen. That is a reasonable concern, but it can be managed with a locked shed, inventory sheet, and clear rules. Parks already require maintenance. Helping volunteers work efficiently is a practical way to support a public space that many residents use.
Passage B: Do Not Add a Tool Shed Yet
The parks department should not add a community tool shed at Henderson Park until it has a stronger maintenance plan. A shed may sound simple, but tools require tracking, cleaning, repair, and replacement. If the department does not manage these tasks consistently, the shed could become an empty or unsafe storage box.
The park already has problems that should come first. Several lights near the walking path are broken, the drinking fountain leaks, and the east playground gate does not close properly. Spending money on volunteer tools before addressing these basic repairs may frustrate residents who have reported safety concerns for months.
Volunteer cleanup groups are valuable, but they can borrow supplies from the parks office for scheduled events. That system is not perfect, but it allows staff to inspect tools before and after use. Before placing equipment permanently at the park, the department should fix existing safety issues, estimate long-term costs, and identify who will be responsible for the shed.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports adding a tool shed to make volunteering easier, while Passage B argues the park should fix safety and maintenance issues first.
choices.B: Passage A argues that volunteers should stop helping, while Passage B argues that parks should be closed.
choices.C: Passage A supports removing all tools from the park, while Passage B supports giving tools away permanently.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on sports leagues, while Passage B focuses on school schedules.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports adding a tool shed to make volunteering easier, while Passage B argues the park should fix safety and maintenance issues first.
questions.explanation: The passages disagree about whether a tool shed is the right immediate priority.

choices.C: The drinking fountain leaks.
choices.D: Tools require tracking, cleaning, repair, and replacement.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some residents do not own tools or cannot carry them on the bus.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s claim that a shed would make volunteering more inclusive.

choices.D: A tool shed would make every cleanup event impossible.
choices.E: The parks office is unable to lend supplies for scheduled events.

questions.correctAnswerText: A tool shed would require ongoing tracking, cleaning, repair, and replacement. | Existing park safety concerns should be addressed before adding a new feature.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports these concerns with specific examples.

choices.A: “Volunteers often arrive ready to remove weeds, spread mulch, or pick up litter.”
choices.B: “A shared tool shed would make volunteer work easier and more inclusive.”
choices.C: “Some people worry that tools will be lost, broken, or stolen.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Some people worry that tools will be lost, broken, or stolen.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly acknowledges a concern from opponents of the tool shed.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the community tool shed.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors value volunteer cleanup work, but they disagree about whether a tool shed is currently the best support.
choices.B: Both authors believe volunteers should bring expensive equipment to every event.
choices.C: Both authors think Henderson Park has no maintenance needs.
choices.D: Both authors argue that residents should stop using the park.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors value volunteer cleanup work, but they disagree about whether a tool shed is currently the best support.
questions.explanation: Passage A supports the shed, while Passage B supports volunteer work but questions the timing and management.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the community tool shed.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: A cost estimate for the shed, tool replacement, staff oversight, and repairs already needed at the park
choices.B: A list of birds seen at Henderson Park in winter
choices.C: A schedule of school holidays in the district
choices.D: A history of the neighborhood’s oldest houses

questions.correctAnswerText: A cost estimate for the shed, tool replacement, staff oversight, and repairs already needed at the park
questions.explanation: This information would help evaluate whether the tool shed is practical compared with other park needs.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Packed in insulated boxes, Maria delivered the lunches to the senior center.

choices.A: Packed in insulated boxes, Maria delivered the lunches to the senior center.
choices.B: Maria delivered the lunches packed in insulated boxes to the senior center.
choices.C: Maria, packed in insulated boxes, delivered the lunches to the senior center.
choices.D: The senior center delivered Maria’s lunches packed in insulated boxes.

questions.correctAnswerText: Maria delivered the lunches packed in insulated boxes to the senior center.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “lunches,” the items packed in insulated boxes.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The clinic mailed a reminder. The reminder listed the appointment date.

choices.A: The clinic mailed a reminder that listed the appointment date.
choices.B: The reminder mailed the clinic and listed the appointment date.
choices.C: Listing the clinic, the appointment date mailed a reminder.
choices.D: The clinic mailed, and the appointment date listed the reminder.

questions.correctAnswerText: The clinic mailed a reminder that listed the appointment date.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The volunteer’s badges were placed on the table before the event.
choices.B: The volunteers badges were placed on the table before the event.
choices.C: The volunteers’ badge’s were placed on the table before the event.
choices.D: The volunteer badges’ were placed on the table before the event.

questions.correctAnswerText: The volunteer’s badges were placed on the table before the event.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the badges belong to one volunteer.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Install Security Cameras in Public Parking Lots
The city should install security cameras in public parking lots near downtown businesses, transit stops, and evening events. These lots are used by workers, shoppers, students, and visitors, often after dark. Several recent reports of vehicle break-ins have made some residents feel unsafe returning to their cars at night. Cameras would not prevent every crime, but they could discourage some theft and provide evidence when incidents occur.
The city can set clear limits to protect privacy. Cameras should face parking rows, payment machines, entrances, and exits, not nearby homes or private windows. Signs should inform drivers that the area is monitored. Footage should be kept only for a limited time unless it is needed for an investigation. A written policy would help prevent misuse.
Security cameras may also support downtown businesses. If customers avoid evening events because they worry about parking-lot safety, restaurants, theaters, and shops lose business. Better lighting, regular patrols, and cameras can work together to make public lots feel safer. The goal is not to watch people unnecessarily; it is to protect public spaces that the city already operates.
Passage B:
Improve Parking Lot Safety Without Cameras
The city should not install security cameras in public parking lots. Safety matters, but cameras can create a false sense of security while raising privacy concerns. A camera may record a break-in, but it does not help a person walking alone to a car if no one is watching the footage in real time. Residents may assume the lot is protected when the actual response remains slow.
The city should first invest in changes that prevent unsafe conditions. Better lighting, trimmed landscaping, clear sight lines, emergency call boxes, and regular foot patrols can make lots safer immediately. These improvements help all users, not just people whose incidents are captured on video. They also avoid collecting footage of ordinary residents going to work, attending appointments, or participating in community events.
Cameras also require long-term costs. The city would need equipment, maintenance, storage, staff access rules, and procedures for sharing footage with law enforcement. Without strong oversight, cameras can be used in ways residents did not expect. Before adding surveillance, the city should improve the physical environment of the lots and evaluate whether those changes reduce problems.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether the city should install security cameras in public parking lots? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
questions.correctAnswerText: Not auto-scored
questions.explanation: Extended Response answers should be reviewed using the rubric guidance. Strong responses make a clear claim, use evidence from both passages, explain why one argument is better supported, and maintain formal organization and language.
